text: Robotic transgastric cyst gastrostomy and pancreatic debridement. Patient presented is a fifty-five-year-old female with history of severe acute pancreatitis of biliary etiology. She had undergone interval cholecystectomy but developed recurrent severe pancreatitis and ultimately a symptomatic retrogastric collection requiring gastrojejunostomy tube placement. CT imaging demonstrated a cystic lesion replacing the body of the pancreas in close apposition to the stomach. There was cystic replacement of the body of the pancreas with residual enhancing tissue in the tail. There is heterogeneous material noted throughout the collection indicating that this is walled off pancreatic necrosis rather than a simple pseudocyst.

The surgeon places a twelve millimeter robotic port at the umbilicus and three additional ports targeting the epigastrium. The assistant port is positioned caudal to the robotic ports in the midclavicular line to minimize instrument collisions. Instruments are then guided into the abdomen under laparoscopic visualization.

The surgeon uses monopolar electrocautery to create an anterior gastrotomy, cutting through the stomach layers until the mucosa is visible. The incision is extended, revealing a large protuberance on the posterior stomach wall. Intraoperative ultrasound is employed to identify the collection, which is then entered with electrocautery, evacuating approximately two liters of fluid.

The surgeon extends the gastrotomy incision to facilitate debridement, removing extensive necrotic tissue from the cavity. Some tissue remains densely adherent to the cavity wall, intended to be auto-debrided by gastric secretions.

The surgeon matures the cyst gastrostomy by suturing the stomach to the cavity wall using two running resorbable barbed sutures anchored at the apices. Following this, the anterior gastrotomy is closed with a single layer of resorbable barbed suture, taking full thickness bites of the gastric wall.

The surgeon completes the gastrotomy closure by tying the suture tails together using a locking plastic clip or running the sutures past the mid portion of the closure. Necrotic tissue is placed into an endoscopic retrieval bag for removal from the abdomen. The closure is reinforced with aerosolized fiber and glue, and no drains are placed.

The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged on post-operative day five. She had resolution of her symptoms and post-operative CT scan demonstrated resolution of her retrogastric fluid collection.
